contentHtml:
Methodology authored by Zach Wade, Wade Intel — operator-led lender tech methodology firm. Validated against production borrower-validation runs at Insignia Capital Corp. · Last reviewed 2026-05-05

Every bridge lender has a story about the borrower who looked great on paper. Good credit. Reasonable LTV. Solid project plan. And then things fell apart — because nobody checked the entity, verified the track record, or searched for prior litigation.

Borrower due diligence is the highest-risk gap in bridge lending. Most lenders still do it manually, inconsistently, or not at all. This guide covers the four pillars of borrower validation and how to build a repeatable process that catches problems before they become losses.

The Four Pillars of Borrower Validation

Every borrower validation should cover these four areas. Skip one and you’re exposed.

1. Entity Validation

The borrowing entity is the legal counterparty to your loan. If that entity is suspended, dissolved, or doesn’t exist, you have a problem.

What to check: – SOS status — Is the entity active and in good standing? Every state maintains a Secretary of State database. An entity that’s suspended for unpaid franchise taxes or dissolved for failure to file annual reports cannot legally transact business. – Formation date — How old is the entity? A brand-new LLC (formed within the last 6 months) applying for a large bridge loan deserves extra scrutiny. – Registered agent — Is the registered agent still active? A resigned agent often indicates neglect or abandonment. – Foreign registration — If the entity is formed in one state but the property is in another, verify the entity is registered to do business in the property state.

Red flags: – Entity formed within 90 days of the loan application – Suspended or not in good standing – Registered agent has resigned – Entity name on application doesn’t match SOS records exactly

The manual version of this check involves visiting up to 50 different state SOS websites, each with a different interface and search process. It takes 5-15 minutes per state, per entity.

2. Track Record Verification

Borrowers self-report their track records. They tell you they’ve done 20 flips, made money on all of them, and never had a problem. The question is: can you verify any of that?

What to check: – Property records — Search property records by the borrower’s name and entity name. How many acquisitions and dispositions show up? Do they match what the borrower claims? – Project types — What kinds of projects has the borrower done? Flips, ground-up construction, buy-and-hold? Match this to what they’re applying to do now. – Outcomes — Of the completed projects, how many were profitable? How many are still held with no activity? How many ended in distress? – Experience tier — Based on verified completions, where does this borrower fall? A borrower with 10+ verified completions is a fundamentally different risk than someone with zero.

Red flags: – Borrower claims 20 projects but property records show 5 – Multiple properties still held years after acquisition with no disposition – Prior projects in a completely different market or project type – No track record under the borrowing entity (all under a different entity or personal name)

3. Litigation Screening

Prior litigation tells you how a borrower handles disputes, obligations, and failure. It’s not that every lawsuit is disqualifying — it’s that undisclosed litigation is a pattern indicator.

What to check: – Federal bankruptcy (PACER) — Has the borrower or entity filed for bankruptcy? Chapter 7 (liquidation) and Chapter 11 (reorganization) are both relevant. Even dismissed cases tell you something. – Foreclosures — Has the borrower lost properties to foreclosure? One foreclosure in a 20-year career may be explainable. Three foreclosures in three years is a pattern. – Active lawsuits — Are there pending lawsuits against the borrower? Construction defect claims, lender lawsuits, partner disputes? – Lis pendens — Are there lis pendens (pending litigation notices) recorded against any of the borrower’s properties? These cloud title and threaten your exit.

Red flags: – Undisclosed bankruptcy (even if discharged) – Active lis pendens on properties currently owned – Pattern of construction defect lawsuits – Multiple foreclosures

4. GC Credential Validation

For construction bridge loans (rehabs, ground-up, value-add), the general contractor is as important as the borrower. An unlicensed, uninsured, or disciplined GC puts the entire project budget at risk.

What to check: – License status — Is the GC’s license active and current in the state where the work will be done? Different states have different licensing requirements. – License classification — Does the license type match the work? A residential handyman license doesn’t cover a $500K commercial rehab. – Disciplinary history — Has the licensing board taken action against this contractor? Citations, fines, and suspensions are public record. – Insurance — Does the GC carry general liability and workers’ compensation insurance? Are the policies current?

Red flags: – Expired or suspended license – License type doesn’t match the scope of work – Active disciplinary actions – No workers’ comp coverage – GC is a related party to the borrower (ownership overlap)

Building a Repeatable Process

The biggest risk in borrower due diligence isn’t missing a single check — it’s inconsistency. When each analyst on your team does checks differently, things get missed.

A repeatable process means:

1. Every borrower gets the same checks. No exceptions for "I know this guy" or "they’ve borrowed from us before." Conditions change. Entities lapse. New lawsuits get filed.

2. Results are documented. When your investor or auditor asks what due diligence you performed, you can show them. Every check type, every data source, every flag.

3. Flags are actionable. A suspended entity isn’t an automatic decline — but it requires a conversation and a documented decision. A flag with no follow-up is worse than no flag at all.

4. The process is fast enough to not skip. If due diligence takes 4 hours per borrower, people will skip it when they’re busy. If it takes 2 minutes, it becomes part of every deal.

The Cost of Getting It Wrong

Bridge loan defaults don’t just cost you the interest you would have earned. They cost:

  • Legal fees — Foreclosure, eviction, litigation defense
  • Carrying costs — Insurance, taxes, maintenance on a property you didn’t plan to own
  • Opportunity cost — Capital tied up in a non-performing asset instead of earning returns
  • Reputation damage — With your investors, your warehouse lender, and in the market

Most of these costs are avoidable with proper borrower validation. The $35-50 cost of a comprehensive validation is trivial compared to the six-figure cost of a single bad loan.

What PulseClose Does

PulseClose automates all four pillars of borrower validation into a single search:

  • Entity validation across all 50 Secretary of State databases
  • Track record verification against property records
  • Litigation screening including federal bankruptcy, foreclosures, and lis pendens
  • GC credential checks including license status and disciplinary history

You enter a borrower name and entity. We run every check in parallel and deliver a structured report with confidence scores, experience tiers, flags, and AI-generated risk analysis — in under two minutes.
